chapter: The Making of the National Movement: 1870s–1947

questions.question: What factors helped the growth of nationalism in India?
questions.answer: Western education, political associations, and criticism of British policies.

questions.question: Why were political associations important in the nineteenth century?
questions.answer: They helped Indians discuss political issues and demand reforms.

questions.question: What was the main aim of the Indian National Congress in its early years?
questions.answer: To present Indian demands and discuss political reforms.

questions.question: Who were the Moderates in the Congress?
questions.answer: Leaders who believed in peaceful and constitutional methods.

questions.question: Name one method used by moderate leaders.
questions.answer: Petitions and appeals to the British government.

questions.question: What did the Drain of Wealth theory explain?
questions.answer: It explained how British policies transferred India's wealth to Britain.

questions.question: Why did Indians oppose the Partition of Bengal?
questions.answer: They believed it was an attempt to divide people and weaken nationalism.

questions.question: What was the main idea of the Swadeshi Movement?
questions.answer: To use Indian-made goods and boycott foreign goods.

questions.question: Name one leader associated with the extremist group.
questions.answer: Bal Gangadhar Tilak.

questions.question: What did extremist leaders demand?
questions.answer: Immediate self-rule or Swaraj.

questions.question: What principle guided Gandhi's political movements?
questions.answer: Truth and nonviolence.

questions.question: What was the objective of the Non-Cooperation Movement?
questions.answer: To oppose British rule by refusing to cooperate with colonial institutions.

questions.question: What happened during the Chauri Chaura incident?
questions.answer: Protesters attacked and burned a police station.

questions.question: Why did Gandhi withdraw the Non-Cooperation Movement?
questions.answer: Because the movement turned violent after the Chauri Chaura incident.

questions.question: What was the aim of the Civil Disobedience Movement?
questions.answer: To challenge British authority by breaking unjust laws peacefully.

questions.question: Why was the Salt March important?
questions.answer: It protested the British tax on salt.

questions.question: Which groups participated in the national movement?
questions.answer: Farmers, workers, students, and women.

questions.question: What was the main demand of the Quit India Movement?
questions.answer: Immediate independence from British rule.

questions.question: Why was the slogan 'Do or Die' important?
questions.answer: It encouraged people to continue the struggle for freedom.

questions.question: How did World War II influence Indian politics?
questions.answer: It weakened British control and strengthened demands for independence.

questions.question: What political changes occurred in 1947?
questions.answer: India gained independence and was partitioned into two countries.

questions.question: What problem arose due to the partition of India?
questions.answer: Large-scale migration and communal violence.

questions.question: Why was unity important during the freedom struggle?
questions.answer: Unity strengthened the resistance against colonial rule.

questions.question: What made the Indian national movement unique?
questions.answer: Its use of nonviolent mass movements.

questions.question: What was the overall goal of the national movement?
questions.answer: To achieve freedom from British rule.
